skill.name: lark-task
skill.description: Manage Lark and Feishu tasks, task lists, assignees, followers, reminders, comments, and completion status through lark-cli commands.
skill.summary: Create, update, search, assign, and organize Lark tasks from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code.
skill.author: larksuite

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review shows the command execution, path traversal, weak crypto, and reconnaissance hits are mostly markdown examples, inline code, sample URLs, and relative documentation links. No malicious prompt injection or data exfiltration intent was found in the reviewed files. The remaining risk is legitimate but meaningful: the skill directs authenticated lark-cli commands that can read and modify live Lark task data.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated External CLI Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to run lark-cli task commands. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it gives the assistant a path to read and change live Lark workspace task data when credentials are available.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files directly document executable lark-cli commands for task reads and writes. The commands match the skill purpose, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Write Actions Modify Live Task State
medium_findings.description: The reference workflows include task creation, updates, comments, assignment changes, reminder changes, completion, reopening, and task list membership changes. The files require confirmation, which reduces but does not remove the risk of unintended workspace changes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed files explicitly identify these workflows as write operations and instruct confirmation before execution. The mitigation is present, but the commands still affect real task records.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The numerous external command detections are caused by fenced bash examples and inline markdown code. No Ruby backtick execution or script file was found in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown documentation and command examples, not Ruby source code. They do not execute by themselves.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Links Misclassified as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The path traversal detections point to relative links such as ../lark-shared/SKILL.md and ../SKILL.md. These are documentation references to shared skill instructions, not filesystem traversal in executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are markdown links and prerequisite notes. There is no file read, write, or user-controlled path construction at those lines.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Flags Lack Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and system reconnaissance detections appear to be keyword matches inside task documentation, dates, IDs, or command descriptions. No cryptographic API, hashing workflow, host probing, or secret discovery behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found task text, sample data, and documented command arguments only. No evidence found for cryptographic misuse or system reconnaissance intent.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Lark URL Is a Sample Task List Link
low_findings.description: The single hardcoded URL is an example Lark AppLink used to show how a task list GUID can be supplied. It is not an external collection endpoint and does not transmit secrets by itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears in a sample command parameter for a Lark task list. There is no code that sends local data to this URL.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CLI Invocation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill relies on lark-cli commands. Marketplace users should understand that execution depends on the local CLI and authenticated workspace permissions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is explicit and repeated across reference files. It is expected behavior for this skill, but it is still a meaningful execution surface.

dangerous_patterns.title: Live Workspace Write Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: Several workflows change tasks, assignments, task lists, comments, reminders, and completion state. The documentation includes confirmation requirements for these write operations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited workflows state that commands should execute after confirmation and identify the operations as writes. This confirms a controlled but real mutation capability.

content.user_title: Manage Lark Tasks from AI Assistants
content.value_statement: Teams often need to create, update, and track Lark tasks without switching tools. This skill provides structured lark-cli workflows for task creation, assignment, reminders, comments, and task lists.
actual_capabilities: Create Lark tasks with summaries, descriptions, due dates, assignees, and task list links.
Search and list tasks assigned to the current user with completion, creation, and due date filters.
Update task summaries, descriptions, due dates, and custom task payload fields.
Complete, reopen, comment on, and assign existing tasks after user confirmation.
Create task lists, add existing tasks to task lists, and manage task list members.
Set or remove task reminders using relative reminder times.
limitations: Requires lark-cli to be installed, authenticated, and configured with the needed Lark permissions.
Does not implement its own API client; it depends on the external lark-cli behavior.
Write actions can change live workspace data and require explicit user confirmation.
Task visibility depends on the current Lark identity and granted scopes.

use_cases.title: Capture Follow-Up Work
use_cases.description: Create assigned Lark tasks with due dates after meetings, reviews, or planning sessions.

use_cases.title: Track Personal Task Status
use_cases.description: Search assigned tasks, review due dates, and mark completed work without leaving the assistant.

use_cases.title: Organize Team Task Lists
use_cases.description: Create shared task lists, add tasks, and manage members for collaborative workstreams.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Task
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Lark task for me called "Review onboarding notes" due tomorrow.

prompt_templates.title: Find My Tasks
prompt_templates.prompt: Show my Lark tasks that mention the product launch and include their due dates and links.

prompt_templates.title: Update a Task Safely
prompt_templates.prompt: Update task t_xxx to be due in two days and add a reminder one hour before it is due.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Task List
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Lark task list for the release checklist, add these five tasks, and assign each task to the listed owner.

output_examples.input: Create a task for me to prepare the weekly metrics review by Friday.
output_examples.output: Created the task "Prepare the weekly metrics review" and assigned it to your Lark user. Due date is Friday. Task link is included for review.

output_examples.input: Find my incomplete tasks about the launch plan.
output: Each result includes the task title, due date, current status, and Lark task link.

output_examples.input: Add Priya as a follower to task t_xxx.
output_examples.output: Confirmed the task and follower change, then added Priya as a follower. The updated follower count was reported.

best_practices: Confirm every write operation before running the command against Lark.
Use dry-run previews when creating or updating tasks with many fields.
Share task links in responses so users can verify the result in Lark.
anti_patterns: Do not run write commands when the user has not confirmed the exact task and change.
Do not guess Lark open_id values; resolve the correct user identity first.
Do not fetch broad task lists when a narrower query or page limit is sufficient.

faq.question: Does this skill require Lark authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires lark-cli to be authenticated with a Lark identity that has the needed task scopes.

faq.question: Can it create and update real tasks?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented commands can create, update, assign, complete, reopen, and comment on live Lark tasks.

faq.question: Can it manage task lists?
faq.answer: Yes. It can create task lists, add tasks to task lists, and manage task list members.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks the skill as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What data can the skill access?
faq.answer: It can access task data available to the authenticated Lark user through lark-cli and the configured permissions.

faq.question: Why does the security audit show medium risk?
faq.answer: The skill uses external CLI commands that can read and change Lark workspace data. This is legitimate, but it needs user confirmation.
